1: אַל-תְּקַנֵּא בְּאַנְשֵׁי הָרָעָה (do not envy the men of evil) — קָנָא (qānāʾ, to be jealous/envious) is the affective disposition that must be actively resisted; the evil man's visible success generates a pull on the viewer. וְאַל-תִּתְאָו לִהְיוֹת אִתָּם (and do not desire to be with them) — the second prohibition targets not just envy of their goods but desire for their company; association is itself the danger. The proverb opens chapter 24's first section with the foundational warning against the attraction of wickedness.
2: כִּי-שֹׁד יֶהְגֶּה לִבָּם (for their heart meditates/murmurs destruction) — שֹׁד (šôd, ruin, devastation, violence) is what the wicked person's lēb (heart) continuously rehearses. הָגָה (hāgâ, to murmur, meditate, utter) is the same verb used of the righteous meditating on Torah (Ps 1:2); here it is applied to the wicked's constant rumination on harm. וְעָמָל שְׂפָתֵיהֶם תְּדַבֵּרְנָה (and their lips speak trouble/toil) — ʿāmāl (toil, mischief, trouble) is what their meditation produces in speech.
3: בְּחׇכְמָה יִבָּנֶה בָּיִת (by wisdom a house is built) — בָּנָה (bānâ, to build) in Niphal; the house (bayit) is both the physical dwelling and the family dynasty. Wisdom is the structural principle of lasting household establishment. וּבִתְבוּנָה יִתְכּוֹנָן (and by understanding it is established) — תְּבוּנָה (təbûnâ, understanding, discernment) from bîn; כּוּן (kôn, to be established, fixed) in the Hithpael — the house establishes itself through understanding, the internalized application of wisdom to lived decisions.
4: וּבְדַעַת חֲדָרִים יִמָּלְאוּ (and by knowledge the rooms are filled) — the house-building metaphor extends: after construction (v. 3) comes furnishing; חֲדָרִים (ḥădārîm, inner rooms, chambers) fill with כָּל-הוֹן יָקָר וְנָעִים (all precious and pleasant wealth). The triad of wisdom-understanding-knowledge (ḥokmâ-təbûnâ-daʿat) builds, establishes, and furnishes the house. The progression mirrors creation: God used wisdom to found the earth (3:19–20).
5: גֶּבֶר-חָכָם בַּעוֹז (a wise man is strong/in strength) — the connection between wisdom and power runs against the intuition that physical strength is primary. גֶּבֶר (geber, a strong man, a warrior) qualified by ḥākām (wise) — wisdom is the warrior's true resource. וְאִישׁ-דַּעַת מְאַמֶּץ-כֹּחַ (and a man of knowledge grows in might) — אָמַץ (ʾāmaṣ, to be strong, to confirm, to grow mighty) in the Piel; knowledge is a power-amplifier, increasing the person's effective capacity beyond raw force.
6: כִּי-בְתַחְבֻּלוֹת תַּעֲשֶׂה-לְּךָ מִלְחָמָה (for by wise guidance you make war for yourself) — תַּחְבֻּלוֹת (taḥbulôt, guidance, steersmanship — from ḥeβel, a rope/cord used in sailing) is the wisdom of strategic direction; the image is nautical before it is military. וּתְשׁוּעָה בְּרֹב יוֹעֵץ (and victory comes with many advisers) — this principle from 11:14 (with identical wording) is repeated; wisdom is distributed in community, not concentrated in one mind. The military context makes the political application concrete.
7: רָאמוֹת לָאֱוִיל חׇכְמוֹת (wisdom is heights/coral to the fool) — רָאמוֹת (rāʾmôt) is debated: "heights" (inaccessible elevations) or "coral" (precious deep-sea material, equally inaccessible). Either way: wisdom is beyond the fool's reach — spatially unattainable. בַּשַּׁעַר לֹא יִפְתַּח-פִּיהוּ (at the gate he will not open his mouth) — the civic gate is the court of public wisdom and justice; the fool's silence there is the practical consequence of wisdom's inaccessibility to him. He has nothing to contribute to the community's deliberation.
8: מְחַשֵּׁב לְהָרַע (whoever plans to do evil) — חָשַׁב (ḥāšab, to think, plan, reckon, devise) in the Piel; intentional, sustained scheming. בַּעַל מְזִמּוֹת יִקְרְאוּ-לוֹ (he will be called a master of schemes) — bʿal məzimmôt (master/owner of schemes); מְזִמָּה (məzimmâ) can be positive (prudent planning) or negative (evil schemes) depending on context; here unambiguously negative. The person becomes identified with his scheming — reputation as a troublemaker attaches to him publicly.
9: זִמַּת אִוֶּלֶת חַטָּאת (the scheme/purpose of folly is sin) — זִמָּה (zimmâ, scheme, plan, evil device) applied to ʾiwwelet (folly); folly is not merely stupid but sinful in its intention. וְתוֹעֲבַת לְאָדָם לֵץ (and the mocker is an abomination to people) — תּוֹעֵבָה (tôʿēbâ, abomination) applied to the lēṣ (scoffer/mocker): what is usually a cultic category (detestable to God) is here applied to the community's social judgment. The mocker evokes the same moral revulsion in humans that abomination evokes in God.
10: הִתְרַפִּיתָ בְּיוֹם צָרָה (if you falter in the day of trouble) — רָפָה (rāpâ, to sink, relax, go limp) in the Hithpael — letting oneself go slack; the day of צָרָה (ṣārâ, trouble, distress, tightness) is precisely when strength is tested. צַר כֹּחֶךָ (your strength is small) — ṣar (narrow, small, cramped) — strength that cannot hold under pressure was always small; adversity reveals what was there all along. The proverb challenges the person to know their real capacity before crisis arrives.
11: הַצֵּל לְקוּחִים לַמָּוֶת (rescue those being taken to death) — לָקַח (lāqaḥ, to take, seize) in the Qal passive participle — those already seized and being led; the urgency is immediate. וּמוֹטִים לַהֶרֶג אִם-תַּחְשׂוֹךְ (and those stumbling toward slaughter, if you hold back) — חָשַׂךְ (ḥāśak, to restrain, withhold) is the sin of inaction when intervention could save life. The proverb mandates active rescue as a moral obligation, not optional generosity.
12: כִּי-תֹאמַר הֵן לֹא-יָדַעְנוּ זֶה (if you say, "We did not know this") — the quoted speech is the self-exculpating claim of willful ignorance; the verse dismantles it by invoking divine omniscience. הֲלֹא-תֹכֵן לִבּוֹת יָבִין (does not he who weighs hearts understand?) — תָּכַן (tākan, to weigh, to measure precisely) applied to the lēb (hearts); God is the precisely calibrated scales of inner intention. וְנֹצֵר נַפְשְׁךָ הוּא יֵדָע (he who guards your nephesh/life — he knows) — nephesh here = life; the one who preserves your existence also observes your moral choices. וְהֵשִׁיב לְאָדָם כְּפָעֳלוֹ (and he will repay each person according to their deed) — the lex talionis principle at divine scale: kəpāʿŏlô (according to their work/deed).
13: אֱכָל-בְּנִי דְבַשׁ כִּי-טוֹב (eat honey, my son, because it is good) — the father-son address returns; the instruction becomes inviting rather than prohibitive. דְּבַשׁ (dəbaš, honey) is the paradigmatic good taste (Ps 19:11; 119:103); וְנֹפֶת מָתוֹק עַל-חִכֶּךָ (and the honeycomb is sweet to your palate) — nōp̄et (honeycomb, dripping honey) intensifies; ḥikkekā (your palate/taste) is the faculty of aesthetic discrimination. The honey sets up the analogy of v. 14.
14: כֵּן דְּעֵה חׇכְמָה לְנַפְשֶׁךָ (so know wisdom for your soul) — the comparative kēn (so, thus) draws the analogy: as honey is good to the taste, so wisdom is good to the nephesh. nephesh here = the inner person, the self that is nourished by wisdom as the body is nourished by honey. אִם-מָצָאתָ וְיֵשׁ אַחֲרִית (if you find it, there is a future) — אַחֲרִית (ʾaḥărît) recurs: the future/outcome that wisdom guarantees. וְתִקְוָתְךָ לֹא תִכָּרֵת (your hope will not be cut off) — identical to 23:18; wisdom's promise is structural: a future that cannot be severed.
15: אַל-תֶּאֱרֹב רָשָׁע לִנְוֵה צַדִּיק (do not lie in wait, O wicked one, against the dwelling of the righteous) — the verse addresses the wicked man directly — a rhetorical shift to second person that implicates anyone tempted to plot against the righteous. אַל-תְּשַׁדֵּד רִבְצוֹ (do not raid his resting place) — שָׁדַד (šādad, to ravage, spoil, destroy) is strong violence; ribṣô (his resting place) is domestic peace. The instruction implies that the righteous man's home is under his Redeemer's protection (cf. 23:11).
16: כִּי שֶׁבַע יִפּוֹל צַדִּיק וָקָם (for the righteous falls seven times and rises again) — שֶׁבַע (šebaʿ, seven) is the number of completeness: however many times the righteous person falls, he rises. נָפַל (fall) and קוּם (rise) are the two poles of the righteous life — not immunity from adversity but resurrection from it. וּרְשָׁעִים יִכָּשְׁלוּ בְרָעָה (but the wicked stumble in calamity) — contrast: the wicked fall when calamity comes and do not rise. The difference is not circumstance but the capacity for recovery that righteousness grants.
17: בִּנְפֹל אוֹיִבְךָ אַל-תִּשְׂמָח (when your enemy falls do not rejoice) — שָׂמַח (śāmaḥ, to rejoice) is forbidden at the enemy's fall; the impulse is natural but the proverb confronts it directly. וּבִכָּשְׁלוֹ אַל-יָגֵל לִבֶּךָ (and when he stumbles do not let your heart exult) — lēb (heart) is the seat of the attitude; even private celebration is wrong. The prohibition reaches inward, not just to public behavior.
18: פֶּן-יִרְאֶה יהוה וְרַע בְּעֵינָיו (lest the LORD see and it be evil in his eyes) — the LORD's gaze is the controlling concern: gloating at the enemy's fall is רַע בְּעֵינָיו (evil in his eyes), the standard of YHWH's moral evaluation. וְהֵשִׁיב מֵעָלָיו אַפּוֹ (and he turns his anger away from your enemy) — the ironic consequence: if you gloat, God redirects his anger from the enemy to you. The enemy's calamity is God's action; appropriating it as your satisfaction perverts its purpose.
19: אַל-תִּתְחַר בַּמְּרֵעִים (do not fret/be agitated because of evildoers) — חָרָה (ḥārâ, to burn, to be angry, to fret) in the Hithpael; self-generating agitation at the wicked, which Ps 37:1 opens identically. אַל-תְּקַנֵּא בָּרְשָׁעִים (do not envy the wicked) — qānāʾ (envy, jealousy) targets their apparent prosperity. Both fretting and envying are responses to perceiving the wicked as having something worth having; the proverb cuts the perception at the root.
20: כִּי לֹא-תִהְיֶה אַחֲרִית לָרָע (for the evil person has no future/outcome) — אַחֲרִית (ʾaḥărît) recurs for the third time in ch. 24 (vv. 14, 18, 20); the chapter organizes itself around the concept of future outcome. The wicked have no lasting future because their prosperity is structurally temporary. נֵר רְשָׁעִים יִדְעָךְ (the lamp of the wicked will be snuffed out) — the lamp (nēr) is a common image for life and household continuation (cf. 13:9; 20:20); its extinction is the end of legacy as well as life.
21: יְרָא-אֶת-יהוה בְּנִי וָמֶלֶךְ (fear the LORD, my son, and the king) — the father-son address returns for the final instruction of the "Words of the Wise" collection. The dual command pairs YHWH and king; both represent legitimate authorities that wisdom honors. עִם-שׁוֹנִים אַל-תִּתְעָרָב (do not associate with those who seek change/are different) — שׁוֹנִים (šônîm) — those who are different, who rebel against established order; עָרַב (ʿārab, to mix, to become surety, to associate) in the Hithpael warns against social mixing with the dangerously unstable.
22: כִּי-פִתְאֹם יָקוּם אֵידָם (for suddenly their disaster will rise) — פִּתְאֹם (pitʾôm, suddenly, unexpectedly) marks the characteristic swiftness of divine judgment on the rebellious; no warning, no gradual approach. וּפִיד שְׁנֵיהֶם מִי יוֹדֵעַ (and the ruin/calamity of both — who knows?) — פִּיד (pîd, ruin, calamity, destruction) is a rare word (cf. Job 12:5; 30:12, 24; 31:29); its scope extends to both king and rebel: both can bring sudden ruin. The uncertainty (mî yôdēaʿ, who knows?) underscores the unpredictability of those who hold power without wisdom.
23: גַּם-אֵלֶּה לַחֲכָמִים (these also are for the wise) — the superscription marks the opening of a second sub-collection (24:23–34), distinct from the "Thirty Sayings" of 22:17–24:22. הַכֵּר-פָּנִים בְּמִשְׁפָּט בַּל-טוֹב (to show partiality in judgment is not good) — הַכֵּר פָּנִים (to recognize the face, to show partiality) is the idiom for favoring based on appearance or social status rather than justice. Judicial impartiality is foundational to the wisdom tradition's ethics (Lev 19:15; Deut 16:19).
24: אֹמֵר לְרָשָׁע צַדִּיק אָתָּה (whoever says to the wicked, "You are righteous") — the false verdict in court; calling the guilty innocent inverts the entire judicial order. יִקְּבֻהוּ עַמִּים (peoples will curse him) — קָבַב (qābab, to curse, utter a curse against) — the universal social consequence: not just one wronged party but ʿammîm (peoples) condemn the corrupt judge. יִזְעָמוּהוּ לְאֻמִּים (nations will denounce him) — lēʾummîm (peoples, nations) parallel ʿammîm; the scope is universal: unjust acquittal of the wicked is an offense against humanity, not merely one case.
25: וְלַמּוֹכִיחִים יִנְעָם (but those who rebuke/reprove will find delight) — יָכַח (yākaḥ, to rebuke, reprove, argue a case, adjudicate) in the Hiphil participle — those who exercise the prophetic-judicial function of honest reproof. עֲלֵיהֶם תָּבוֹא בִרְכַּת-טוֹב (upon them will come a blessing of good) — the passive of divine blessing (tābôʾ, will come) settles on those who speak truth against the wicked. The good-blessing (birkat-ṭôb) is the counterpart to the peoples' curse on the corrupt judge.
26: שְׂפָתַיִם יִשָּׁק (he will kiss the lips) — the metaphor is one of the most striking in Proverbs: an honest answer is like a kiss. מֵשִׁיב דְּבָרִים נְכֹחִים (one who gives back right/direct words) — נָכוֹחַ (nākôaḥ, right, straight, before one) describes speech that goes directly where it should without deflection. The kiss metaphor suggests warmth, intimacy, and trust; honest speech creates and sustains the bond that sycophancy destroys.
27: הָכֵן בַּחוּץ מְלַאכְתֶּךָ (prepare your outdoor work) — כּוּן (kôn, to establish, prepare, make ready) in the Hiphil imperative; outdoor work (ḥûṣ, outside, field) comes first. וְאַחַר בָּנִיתָ בֵיתֶךָ (and afterward build your house) — the sequence is economic prudence: establish productive capacity before consuming it in construction. The proverb echoes Jesus's parable of the builder counting costs (Luke 14:28–30); wisdom calculates before committing.
28: אַל-תִּהְיֶה עֵד-חִנָּם בְּרֵעֶךָ (do not be a witness without cause against your neighbor) — חִנָּם (ḥinnām, for nothing, without cause, groundlessly) modifies the witness role; groundless testimony is the legal wrong named. וַהֲפִתִּיתָ בִּשְׂפָתֶיךָ (and do not deceive with your lips) — פָּתָה (pātâ, to open, to deceive, to entice) — verbal deception in legal context; the prohibition extends from false testimony to any form of speech designed to mislead in matters affecting another's legal standing.
29: אַל-תֹּאמַר אֶעֱשֶׂה-לוֹ כַּאֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה-לִי (do not say, "I will do to him as he did to me") — the quoted speech is the voice of human retributive instinct; the proverb forbids it by forbidding even its formulation in speech. אָשִׁיב לָאִישׁ כְּפָעֳלוֹ (I will repay the man according to his deed) — the self-assumed role of judge and avenger; kəpāʿŏlô (according to his deed) is exactly YHWH's prerogative (24:12). Taking it for oneself usurps the divine role. The New Testament's prohibition on revenge (Rom 12:17–19) has this exact proverb as background.
30: עַל שְׂדֵה אִישׁ-עָצֵל עָבַרְתִּי (I passed by the field of a lazy man) — the poem shifts to autobiographical first-person narrative, the sage as observer. וְעַל-כֶּרֶם אָדָם חֲסַר-לֵב (and by the vineyard of a man who lacked heart/sense) — חֲסַר-לֵב (ḥăsar-lēb, lacking heart, senseless) — the lēb (heart/mind) is the organ of wisdom; to lack it is to be constitutionally incapable of prudent planning. The field and vineyard are read as diagnostic landscapes.
31: וְהִנֵּה עָלָה כֻלּוֹ קִמְּשֹׂנִים (and behold, it was all overgrown with thorns) — the wəhinnēh (and behold) signals the vivid, surprising scene that confronts the observer. קִמָּשׂוֹן (qimmāśôn, weeds, nettles, thorns) — a term for invasive vegetation that colonizes untended ground. כִּסּוּ פָנָיו חֲרֻלִּים (nettles covered its face) — pānāyw (its face) is the surface of the field; ḥarullîm (nettles, weeds) have taken the face. וְגֶדֶר אֲבָנָיו נֶהֱרָסָה (and its stone wall was broken down) — the infrastructure of order (wall = property boundary + protection) has collapsed along with the crop.
32: וָאֶחֱזֶה אָנֹכִי אָשִׁית לִבִּי (I saw it, I took it to heart) — the observer's response: אָשִׁית לִבִּי (I set my heart) — active attention, not passive seeing. רָאִיתִי לָקַחְתִּי מוּסָר (I looked and received instruction) — לָקַח מוּסָר (to receive/take instruction) is the phrase for wisdom's appropriation (cf. 1:3; 8:10); the sage reads the landscape as a moral text. The neglected field is the medium of instruction; observation becomes formation.
33: מְעַט שֵׁנוֹת מְעַט תְּנוּמוֹת (a little sleep, a little slumber) — the triple diminutive (מְעַט, məʿaṭ, a little) is ironic; each "little" compounds. מְעַט חִבֻּק יָדַיִם לִשְׁכָּב (a little folding of hands to rest) — חָבַק יָדַיִם (to fold the hands) is the posture of inaction; the hands that should work are crossed in comfortable rest. Identical to 6:10, where the same picture opens the warning about the sluggard's self-destruction. The repetition of a known text signals that the wise man has absorbed a prior instruction and sees it confirmed in the field before him.
34: וּבָא-מִתְהַלֵּךְ רֵישֶׁךָ (then poverty will come on you like a wanderer) — מִתְהַלֵּךְ (mitəhallēk, Hithpael participle of hālak, to walk) — poverty that walks toward you like a drifter, arriving inevitably. וּמַחְסֹרְךָ כְּאִישׁ מָגֵן (and your need like an armed man) — māgēn is often "shield" but here the armed defender figure suggests poverty arriving with military force, not to be denied entry. Identical to 6:11; the poem ends with the same warning with which it began in chapter 6. The sage has observed it in the world and confirms what wisdom had already taught.
